quizTitle: CPL(H) Aircraft General Knowledge Practice Exam

questions.question: The most likely cause of a high oil temperature and low oil pressure is:
questions.explanation: Low oil level can lead to insufficient lubrication and cooling of engine components, resulting in increased friction and heat generation. This can cause the oil temperature to rise while the oil pressure drops due to the reduced volume of oil circulating through the engine.
questions.kd: Unit 1.1.1   3.2.(c) how to identify different grades of aviation fuel

questions.question: In an aircraft without the engine operating, the reading on a manifold pressure gauge at sea level under ISA conditions is:
questions.explanation:
In an aircraft without the engine operating, the manifold pressure gauge will read:
Approximately 29.92 inches of mercury (inHg) at sea level under ISA conditions.
The manifold pressure gauge measures absolute pressure in the intake manifold. With the engine not running, there’s no suction created by piston movement, therefore the manifold pressure equals ambient atmospheric pressure. In an aircraft without the engine operating, the manifold pressure gauge will read approximately 29.92 inches of mercury (inHg) at sea level under ISA conditions.

questions.question: Which rotor system/s can mast bumping occur with:
questions.explanation: Mast bumping occurs primarily in semi-rigid (teetering) rotor systems. This condition arises when excessive blade flapping causes the rotor hub to strike the mast, typically due to low-G maneuvers, abrupt cyclic inputs, or turbulence.

questions.question: Which rotor system/s can ground resonance occur with:
questions.explanation: Ground resonance can occur in fully articulated rotor systems. If the rotor blades become out of phase (uneven lead-lag positions), and the helicopter is on the ground, this can cause a resonant vibration between the airframe and rotor system.

questions.question: In an aircraft without the engine operating, the reading on a manifold pressure gauge at 2000ft above mean sea level under ISA conditions is:
questions.explanation: At 2000 feet above mean sea level under ISA conditions, with the engine not operating (throttle open), the manifold pressure gauge would read approximately 28 inches of mercury (inHg). Standard sea level pressure: 29.92 inHg and pressure drops by about 1 inHg per 1000 feet.

questions.question: Fuel boost pump/s:
options: provide additional fuel for take-off and landing
provide additional fuel in an emergency
can only be used at altitude
questions.explanation: The fuel boost pump is designed to maintain fuel pressure and prevent vapour lock, especially during high power settings or at high altitudes where fuel can vaporise due to lower atmospheric pressure.

questions.question: The colour of 100LL (100 octane low lead) aviation fuel is:
questions.explanation: The colour of 100LL (100 octane low lead) aviation fuel is blue. This colour coding helps prevent fuel contamination and misfuelling errors.
questions.kd: Unit 1.1.1   3.2.(c) how to identify different grades of aviation fuel

questions.question: The colour of 100/130 aviation fuel is:
questions.explanation: The colour of 100/130 aviation fuel is green. This colour coding helps prevent fuel contamination and misfuelling errors.
questions.kd: Unit 1.1.1   3.2.(c) how to identify different grades of aviation fuel

questions.question: Carburettor icing can only occur if the outside ambient temperature is less than 0˚C
questions.explanation: Carburettor icing can occur even when the outside air temperature is well above 0°C, typically between -5°C to +30°C (23°F to 86°F). Especially when Humidity is high (above 50% relative humidity)

questions.question: Which flight condition would most likely cause carburettor icing?
options: on departure with a high rate of climb
on descent during a steep approach
questions.explanation: A steep descent with low power is a prime condition for carburettor icing, especially if the weather is moist and cool. During descent, throttle is often reduced, leading to lower air pressure and temperature inside the carburettor.

questions.question: Battery capacity is measured in:
questions.explanation: Battery capacity is measured in ampere-hours (Ah). An ampere-hour represents the amount of current (in amperes) a battery can deliver over one hour.

questions.question: Descending with a blocked pitot tube, the ASI will:
questions.explanation: Remember: PUDSUC (Pitot blocked, underread on descent - Static blocked under read on climb)

questions.question: Climbing with a blocked pitot tube, the ASI will:
questions.explanation: Remember: PUDSUC (Pitot blocked, underread on descent - Static blocked under read on climb)

questions.question: Descending with a blocked static tube, the ASI will:
questions.explanation: Remember: PUDSUC (Pitot blocked, underread on descent - Static blocked under read on climb)

questions.question: Climbing with a blocked static  tube, the ASI will:
questions.explanation: Remember: PUDSUC (Pitot blocked, underread on descent - Static blocked under read on climb)

questions.question: Descending with a blocked static tube, the Altimeter will:
questions.explanation: A blocked static tube will cause the altimeter to under read during descent and over read during climb, as it will not accurately measure the static pressure.

questions.question: Climbing with a blocked static tube, the VSI will:
questions.explanation: The VSI is solely reliant on the rate of change of static pressure, therefore if the static system is blocked the VSI will not function and read 0

questions.question: Descending with a blocked static tube, the VSI will:
questions.explanation: The VSI is solely reliant on the rate of change of static pressure, therefore if the static system is blocked the VSI will not function and read 0

questions.question: Descending with a blocked pitot tube, the VSI will:
questions.explanation: The VSI is solely reliant on the rate of change of static pressure and is not connected to the pitot system. Therefore if the pitot system is blocked the VSI will function normally. Remember the pitot tube is only used for the ASI.

questions.question: Climbing with a blocked pitot tube, the VSI will:
questions.explanation: The VSI is solely reliant on the rate of change of static pressure and is not connected to the pitot system. Therefore if the pitot system is blocked the VSI will function normally. Remember the pitot tube is only used for the ASI.

questions.question: A rate one turn is:
options: a turn that takes 30 seconds to complete a full 360˚ turn
a turn that takes 1 minute to complete a full 360˚ turn
a turn that takes 2 minutes to complete a full 360˚ turn
a turn that takes 3 minutes to complete a full 360˚ turn
questions.explanation: A rate one is a turn in which the aircraft changes heading at 3˚ per second, completing a full 360˚ turn in 2 minutes.

questions.question: A check valve in a hydraulic system:
options: is a valve that checks the flow of hydraulic fluid
a valve which restricts the flow of fluid to one direction
regulates the pressure of a hydraulic system
questions.explanation: A check valve in a hydraulic system is a device that allows fluid to flow in one direction only and prevents backflow in the opposite direction. It operates automatically and does not require external control.

questions.question: A 100kg force applied to a hydraulic piston with an area of 10cm2 will produce a force of:
questions.explanation: 100kg divide by 10cm2 = 10kg per square centremetre

questions.question: AC power produced by an alternator is converted to DC power by which component?
questions.explanation: AC power produced by an alternator is converted to DC power by a rectifier. The rectifier typically consists of diodes arranged to allow current to flow in only one direction, converting the alternating current (AC) output from the alternator to direct current (DC).

questions.question: A 500kg force applied to a hydraulic piston with an area of 20cm2 will produce a force of:
questions.explanation: 500kg divide by 20cm2 = 25kg per square centimetre

questions.question: The 4 stroke Otto cycle on a piston engine is:
questions.explanation: Intake → Compression → Power → Exhaust This is the complete 4-stroke cycle used in many aircraft piston engines.

questions.question: During flight, the alternator fails and the aircraft battery is rated at 20 amp-hours. If your electrical load is 10 amps, how long can you expect the battery to supply power?
questions.explanation: This is the complete 4-stroke cycle used in many aircraft piston engines.

questions.question: DC power is converted to AC power by which component?
questions.explanation: DC power is converted to AC power by an inverter. An inverter takes direct current (DC) and converts it into alternating current (AC) at the required frequency and voltage for the aircraft's AC-powered systems.

questions.question: Your centre zero ammetre is indicating zero ampres, this indicates:
options: the alternator output power equals the electrical load demand on the aircraft
questions.explanation: If your centre-zero ammeter is indicating zero amps, this typically indicates that the electrical system is balanced — the alternator or generator is supplying exactly what the aircraft’s electrical load is demanding, with no excess to charge the battery.

questions.question: Your centre zero ammetre is indicating a slightly high ampres reading, this indicates:
options: the electrical load demand on the aircraft exceeds the alternator output power
questions.explanation: A slightly high positive ammeter reading shows the battery is charging, which is normal after engine start.

questions.question: What is the effect on the power output of the engine after applying carburettor heat?
questions.explanation: When carburettor heat is applied, the power output of the engine typically decreases. Warmer air is less dense than cold air and this reduces the amount of oxygen entering the engine; Less oxygen means less fuel can be burned efficiently, reducing power.
questions.kd: Unit 1.1.1   3.4.8 State the effect of the application of carburettor heat on engine performance and engine instument indications

questions.question: A delta-3 hinge:
options: connects the tail rotor drive shaft to the tail rotor gearbox
is an offset flapping hinge that reduces the amount of flap back
are found on fenestron tail rotors only
questions.explanation: A Delta-3 hinge increases stability by automatically reducing excessive flapping of the rotor blades.

questions.question: A fully articulated rotor system can:
options: flap up and down, lead and lag
questions.explanation: A fully articulated rotor system can allow each rotor blade to move independently in three ways. Flap — Up and down movement to compensate for dissymmetry of lift. Lead-lag (drag) — Forward and backward movement in the plane of rotation to absorb changes in rotational speed due to varying aerodynamic forces. Feather — Rotation around the blade’s longitudinal axis to change the blade pitch angle.

questions.question: What is the primary function of a carburettor in a piston-engine helicopter?
options: To mix oil with air for lubrication
To mix fuel and air in the correct ratio for combustion
To deliver pressurised fuel to the fuel injectors
questions.explanation: The primary function of a carburettor in a piston-engine helicopter is to mix the correct ratio of fuel and air for combustion in the engine.

questions.question: Carburettor icing is most likely to occur under which of the following conditions?
options: High power settings, hot and dry air
Low power settings, humid and cool air
Cruise power, cold and dry air

questions.question: What is the purpose of the carburettor heat control?
options: To cool the intake air to improve engine performance
To prevent overheating of the fuel pump
To direct warm air into the carburettor to prevent or remove ice
To increase fuel flow to the cylinders
questions.explanation: The purpose of the carburettor heat control is to prevent or remove carburettor icing by warming the air entering the carburettor. The carb heat control diverts hot air from around the engine exhaust into the carburettor intake.

questions.question: When carburettor heat is applied, what is the expected immediate effect on engine power?
options: Increase in power due to richer mixture
Decrease in power due to warmer, less dense air
questions.explanation: Carburettor heat introduces warmer, less dense air into the engine. Warmer air reduces the air density, leading to a richer fuel-air mixture and results in a power reduction.
questions.kd: Unit 1.1.1   3.4.8 State the effect of the application of carburettor heat on engine performance and engine instument indications

questions.question: What is the purpose of a fuel boost pump in a helicopter fuel system?
options: To maintain fuel pressure and prevent vapour lock
To provide additional boost during the take-off sequence
To remove contaminants from the fuel
To prevent over-pressurisation of the fuel lines
questions.explanation: The fuel boost pump ensures a reliable and uninterrupted fuel supply, enhancing engine performance and safety during critical flight operations. It also Ppevent fuel vaporisation (vapour lock) at high temperatures or altitudes.

questions.question: In a gravity-fed fuel system, what is essential for uninterrupted fuel flow?
options: Fuel tanks located above the engine
A vapour return line to the tank

questions.question: What component stores hydraulic pressure for emergency use in some systems?
questions.explanation: The accumulator ensures hydraulic pressure is available for emergency or backup operations in case of system failure. It stores hydraulic fluid under pressure, often using a compressed gas like nitrogen on one side of a diaphragm or piston. In the event of a hydraulic pump failure, the accumulator provides emergency hydraulic power to operate critical systems like brakes, landing gear, or flight controls.

questions.question: What is the function of the hydraulic reservoir?
options: To allow thermal expansion and hold excess fluid
To cool the fluid before delivery
To filter out vapour from the fluid
questions.explanation: The hydraulic reservoir stores, cools, and conditions hydraulic fluid to ensure efficient and reliable system operation.

questions.question: What does a centre-zero ammeter indicate in a helicopter electrical system?
options: Rate of charge or discharge of the battery
questions.explanation: A center-zero ammeter in a helicopter electrical system indicates the direction and magnitude of current flow — whether the battery is being charged or discharged.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a left-hand-zero ammeter?
options: To measure both voltage and amperage
To indicate total current flow from the alternator
To show battery state of charge

questions.question: In which of the following scenarios would a centre-zero ammeter read zero during flight?
options: The alternator is operating and no battery charging or discharge is occurring
The battery is being discharged rapidly
The engine has been shut down
questions.explanation: A center-zero ammeter in a helicopter electrical system indicates the direction and magnitude of current flow — whether the battery is being charged or discharged. Therefore if the ammeter is reading zero, the battery is neither charging or discharging.

questions.question: The starter motor is operated by:
questions.explanation: The starter motor is typically powered by the battery. When the ignition switch is turned to the start position, it allows current from the battery to flow to the starter motor, which then engages with the engine's flywheel to crank the engine and start it.

questions.question: Detonation in a piston engine occurs:
questions.explanation: Detonation occurs when the fuel-air mixture auto-ignites violently, causing damaging shock waves inside the engine. The unburned air-fuel mixture in the combustion chamber explodes violently instead of burning smoothly.
questions.kd: Unit 1.1.1  3.3.1 State the causes and effects of detonation, limited to improper use of mixture control. MP/RPM and use of incorrect fuel octane

questions.question: What is the typical effect of a broken P-lead (primary lead) in a magneto system?
options: The magneto will stop functioning completely
The magneto will remain live and continue to fire
The ignition switch will lock in place
questions.explanation: A broken or disconnected P-lead prevents grounding of the magneto, so it remains live and continues producing spark even when the ignition is switched off.
questions.kd: Unit 1.2.9   2.2.1(c)(viii) dual ignition distributor ignition switch

questions.question: During a magneto check at run-up, the pilot notices no RPM drop when selecting either magneto. What is the most likely cause?
options: The ignition switch is in the wrong position
One or both P-leads are open or disconnected
questions.kd: Unit 1.2.9   2.2.1(c)(viii) dual ignition distributor ignition switch

questions.question: How is a magneto typically disabled during engine shutdown?
options: By grounding the P-leads through the ignition switch
By opening the secondary coil circuit

questions.question: What is the primary function of a fuel tank vent?
options: To prevent water from entering the tank
To allow fuel to drain from the system
To maintain atmospheric pressure inside the tank
To supply fuel to the injector pump
questions.explanation: The primary function of a fuel tank vent is to allow air to enter and exit the fuel tank to maintain proper pressure balance as fuel levels change.

questions.question: Which of the following conditions is most likely to result in fuel starvation despite adequate fuel quantity?
What is the likely result of a blocked fuel tank vent during flight?
options: The fuel system will over-pressurise and burst
Fuel flow may stop due to vacuum formation in the tank
The engine will run excessively rich

questions.question: What acceleration error will the compass experience indicate during an acceleration on an easterly heading in the Southern Hemisphere?
questions.explanation: In the Southern Hemisphere, during acceleration on an easterly heading, the magnetic compass will indicate a turn to the south. This is due to the acceleration error caused by the magnetic dip — the vertical component of Earth's magnetic field pulls the compass card downward.

questions.question: Which compass error is most affected when flying near the magnetic poles?
questions.explanation: The compass error most affected when flying near the magnetic poles is dip error, also known as magnetic dip or vertical component error. This leads to increased turning errors and acceleration errors

questions.question: What is the main cause of magnetic dip?
options: The vertical component of Earth's magnetic field
questions.explanation: The main cause of magnetic dip is the inclination of Earth's magnetic field lines, which are not parallel to the Earth's surface except at the magnetic equator.

questions.question: What acceleration error will the compass experience indicate during an acceleration on an westerly heading in the Southern Hemisphere?
What acceleration error will the compass experience indicate during an acceleration on an southerly heading in the Southern Hemisphere?
What acceleration error will the compass experience indicate during  an acceleration on an northerly heading in the Southern Hemisphere?
What is the main disadvantage of a vacuum-powered attitude indicator compared to an electric one?
Which flight condition is most likely to lead to impact icing?
questions.explanation: Visible moisture is required for impact icing to form

questions.question: What is the most likely cause of a fluctuating CHT (cylinder head temperature) gauge at a rapid rate?
questions.explanation: A rapidly fluctuating Cylinder Head Temperature (CHT) gauge is most likely caused by a faulty CHT sensor (thermocouple) or a poor electrical connection (loose wiring, broken lead). The CHT gauge relies on a thermocouple sensor mounted on the cylinder head and if this sensor or wiring is intermittent or damaged, it causes erratic or rapid fluctuations in the gauge reading. Actual engine temperature usually changes slowly, so rapid fluctuations are typically electrical or sensor faults.

questions.question: Where are fire bottles usually discharged?
questions.kd: Unit 1.2.9   2.2.1(g) types of extinguishers and usages

questions.question: What is the basic operating principle of a wire loop fire detection system?
options: It uses magnetic fields to sense flame ionisation
It measures pressure fluctuations in the engine compartment
It detects a change in electrical resistance caused by heat
It relies on radiation sensors detecting infrared energy
questions.explanation: The basic operating principle of a wire loop fire detection system is based on monitoring the continuity and resistance within a closed electrical circuit (loop). This system is designed to detect conditions indicative of a fire, such as excessive heat or smoke, by observing changes in the circuit's electrical properties.

questions.question: What is a key advantage of a wire loop fire detection system?
options: It can detect the presence of smoke
It provides rapid and reliable heat detection
It is only active during engine operation

questions.question: How does an IVSI improve upon the standard VSI?
options: It compensates for instrument error using pitot pressure
It uses engine RPM to determine climb rate
It adds an accelerometer to reduce lag
It measures vertical velocity using GPS
questions.explanation: An IVSI uses accelerometers or electronic sensors to detect vertical acceleration, then instantly calculates and displays vertical speed. This reduces the lag time significantly.

questions.question: During a rapid descent, what will a standard VSI typically display at the moment the descent begins?
options: A zero reading that slowly increases
A sharp drop to indicate descent
A spike followed by no reading
questions.explanation: Due to lag, the standard VSI initially shows zero and takes a few seconds to react.

questions.question: The VSI measures:
options: the rate of change of static pressure
the rate of change of static + dynamic pressure
the rate of change of dynamic pressure
the rate of change of the centre of pressure
questions.explanation: The VSI (Vertical Speed Indicator) measures the rate of change of atmospheric pressure, which it converts to the rate of climb or descent of an aircraft, typically displayed in feet per minute (fpm).

questions.question: Which hydraulic system component helps manage excess pressure generated by a constant displacement pump?
questions.explanation: The component in a hydraulic system that manages excess pressure generated by a constant displacement pump is the pressure relief valve. This valve prevents system damage by opening when pressure exceeds a set limit, allowing fluid to bypass back to the reservoir or tank, maintaining safe operating conditions.

questions.question: Some helicopters have a blue line on the airspeed indicator, what is this indicating?
questions.explanation: The blue line on the airspeed indicator indicates the maximum speed at which the helicopter can be safely operated in autorotation.
questions.kd: Unit 1.1.1   3.5.1 Explain the colour code markings on an airspeed indicator (ASI)

questions.question: You are on descent with a blocked pitot tube, your VSI will:
questions.explanation: If only the pitot tube is blocked (and the static port is clear), the VSI will operate normally. This is because the VSI needs static pressure only to indicate vertical speed.
questions.kd: Unit 1.2.9   2.3.3(b)(i) blockage of the static source

questions.question: A semi rigid rotor system can:
options: flap up and down, lead and lag

questions.question: What is the function of the accumulator fitted to a hydraulic system?
options: provides limted pressure after a system failure
questions.explanation: The function of an accumulator in a hydraulic system is to Store hydraulic fluid under pressure and release it when needed.

questions.question: In an aircraft without the engine operating, the reading on a manifold pressure gauge at 3000ft above mean sea level under ISA conditions is:
The rotor brake is used for:
options: assist in the event of a tail rotr failure
only ever used in an emergency

questions.question: While climbing at full throttle in an aircraft with a manually controlled wastegate, what is the correct action to maintain constant manifold pressure for as long as possible?
options: Slowly open the wastegate to increase flow

questions.question: During engine run-up, turning off the right magneto causes no change in RPM, but switching off the left magneto causes the engine to stop. What does this suggest?
options: The right magneto is still live

questions.question: If signs of fuel vapourisation are detected during flight, what is the most appropriate immediate pilot action?
options: Move the mixture to full rich
questions.explanation: Immediate actions for fuel vapourisation include activating the auxiliary fuel pump, switching tanks, enriching the mixture, and descending to cooler, denser air, while closely monitoring the engine's performance.

questions.question: Under which of the following conditions is fuel vapourisation most likely to occur during high power operation?
options: When the fuel is hot and ambient pressure is low
When the fuel is cold and ambient pressure is high
When the fuel is cold and ambient pressure is low
When the fuel is hot and ambient pressure is high

questions.question: A fully rigid rotor system can:
options: flap up and down, lead and lag
questions.explanation: A fully rigid rotor system allows for feathering, flapping, and lead-lag through blade flexing, enabling precise and agile control but with increased structural demands.

questions.question: The yellow band on the CAT (carburettor Air Temperature) gauge indicates:
questions.explanation: The yellow band on the CAT (Carburettor Air Temperature) gauge indicates the temperature range where carburettor icing is most likely to occur.

questions.question: A voltmeter in an aircraft reads 24 volts before engine start. What would be a normal voltage indication in cruise flight?
questions.explanation: The battery voltage at rest is about 24 V (for a 24-volt system). When the engine is running and the alternator/generator is supplying power and charging the battery, the voltage will be slightly higher than battery voltage to allow charging. So, a typical charging voltage range is around 26–28 volts on a 24-volt system.

questions.question: What is the purpose of an oil cooler bypass in an aircraft engine lubrication system?
options: To allow oil to bypass the cooler if it becomes obstructed
To prevent oil from flowing through the cooler when it's already cold
To redirect overheated oil to the cooler again

questions.question: If carburettor heat is applied due to suspected icing and the engine begins to run rough, what is the correct pilot response?
options: Leave carburettor heat on to allow ice to melt
Lean the mixture as the application of carburettor heat enrichens it
Richen the mixture to restore smooth engine operation
Turn off carburettor heat and descend to warmer air
questions.explanation: If carburettor heat is applied due to suspected carburettor icing and the engine begins to run rough, the correct pilot response is to leave the carburettor heat ON. It happens because the carb heat melts the ice, and the water from the melted ice is ingested by the engine, causing temporary roughness.

questions.question: During cruise flight, an aircraft fitted with a centre-zero ammeter shows a reading slightly below zero. What does this indicate?
options: The electrical load is greater than the generator output
The battery is fully charged and the generator is charging it
The electrical load equals the generator output
The electrical load is less than the generator output
questions.explanation: A center-zero ammeter in an aircraft shows the net current flowing to or from the battery below zero (negative side) means current is flowing from the battery — the battery is discharging.

questions.question: When one magneto is switched off in a dual-ignition system, what is the most likely effect on engine operation?
options: Only one spark plug fires, causing slower combustion and reduced peak cylinder pressure
The ignition timing advances, bringing peak pressure earlier in the stroke
Engine power increases due to reduced electrical load
The ignition timing is effectively retarded, so peak pressure occurs later in the power stroke
questions.explanation: In a dual-ignition system, when one magneto is switched off, the engine will continue to operate but with a slight drop in RPM or power output. Switching off one magneto results in each cylinder firing with only one spark plug, reducing combustion efficiency slightly.

questions.question: If an aircraft engine is operated at idle speed for an extended period, what is a likely consequence?
options: The spark plugs will become inoperative
The piston rings may show excessive wear
The spark plugs may become fouled

questions.question: What is the primary function of an impulse coupling on a magneto?
options: May cause pre-ignition if it produces too hot a spark for the engine
Produces a ‘hot’ spark for easier starting by momentarily increasing magneto speed
Can be either a high-tension or low-tension type
Produces easier stopping of the engine
questions.explanation: The impulse coupling helps produce a hot, well-timed spark for starting the engine when the crankshaft is turning slowly.
questions.kd: Unit 1.2.9   2.2.1(c)(viii) dual ignition distributor ignition switch

questions.question: Calibrated Airspeed (CAS) is:
options: IAS corrected for altitude and temperature
IAS corrected for position and instrument errors
questions.explanation: Calibrated Airspeed (CAS) is the indicated airspeed (IAS) corrected for instrument errors and position (installation) errors.

questions.question: True Airspeed (TAS) is defined as:
options: The airspeed corrected for instrument error
The airspeed corrected for altitude and temperature effects
The speed shown directly on the airspeed indicator
The aircraft’s speed relative to the ground
questions.explanation: TAS reflects the true velocity of the aircraft through the air, regardless of altitude or environmental conditions.

questions.question: At high altitudes, which airspeed will typically be much higher than IAS?
questions.explanation: At high altitudes, True Airspeed (TAS) will typically be much higher than Indicated Airspeed (IAS). As altitude increases air density decreases, which causes IAS to read lower for the same actual speed. TAS increases because the aircraft must fly faster through the thinner air to generate the same lift.

questions.question: An altimeter uses which of the following components to measure altitude?
What is the standard sea level pressure setting on an altimeter (ISA)?
questions.explanation: The standard sea level pressure setting on an altimeter according to the International Standard Atmosphere (ISA) is 1013hPa

questions.question: What will the altimeter indicate if the pressure setting is LOWER than actual sea level pressure?
options: Indicated altitude will be too high
Indicated altitude will be too low
questions.explanation: The altimeter measures pressure to determine altitude, assuming the standard atmospheric pressure gradient. If the setting is too low, the instrument interprets the current pressure as being higher up in the atmosphere than it really is.

questions.question: Which factor does NOT directly affect an altimeter reading?
What physical principle allows the artificial horizon to maintain its orientation relative to the Earth's surface?
questions.explanation: The gyroscope in the artificial horizon maintains its orientation due to rigidity in space, meaning the gyro resists changes to its axis of rotation, providing a stable reference for pitch and bank angles.

questions.question: What powers a typical vacuum-driven artificial horizon in small aircraft?
questions.explanation: In many small aircraft, the artificial horizon is powered by a vacuum pump that drives the gyroscope, although electric versions are common in modern systems.

questions.question: What will typically cause the artificial horizon to "tumble"?
options: Loss of vacuum or electrical power
questions.explanation: If the vacuum system fails (or power in electric gyros is lost), the gyroscope may lose its stability, causing the horizon display to tumble or become unreliable.

questions.question: Unlike the Turn and Slip Indicator, the Turn Coordinator is sensitive to which aircraft movement?
questions.explanation: The Turn Coordinator’s gyro is canted (tilted), making it sensitive to both yaw and roll, providing an earlier indication of the aircraft entering a bank.

questions.question: In a coordinated turn, where will the ball in the inclinometer be?
questions.explanation: A centered ball indicates a properly coordinated turn, balancing centrifugal force with the horizontal component of lift.

questions.question: What is the primary principle behind the operation of a mechanical direction indicator (heading indicator)?
questions.explanation: The direction indicator operates on the principle of gyroscopic rigidity, where the spinning rotor maintains a fixed orientation in space, providing a stable reference for heading information.

questions.question: Why does a direct reading compass have a 'lubber line'?
options: To indicate the level of fluid inside
To display the aircraft's current magnetic heading
To mark the location of compensating magnets
To prevent the compass from overheating
questions.explanation: The lubber line is a fixed reference mark that shows the aircraft's current heading relative to the rotating compass card.
questions.kd: Unit 1.2.9   2.5.1 Describe the princiuple and construction of a magnetica compass

questions.question: What type of error is caused by the vertical component of Earth's magnetic field on a direct reading compass?
questions.explanation: Compass dip occurs because the Earth's magnetic field has a vertical component, especially near the poles, which can cause the compass card to tilt and lead to errors.
questions.kd: Unit 1.2.9   2.5.1 Describe the princiuple and construction of a magnetica compass

questions.question: What is the primary advantage of coupling a directional indicator with a flux valve?
options: It eliminates the need for a magnetic compass entirely
It automatically corrects for gyroscopic drift
It removes the need for a vacuum system
questions.explanation: A flux valve senses the Earth's magnetic field and feeds this data to the Directional Gyro (DI), allowing it to automatically correct for drift, keeping the heading indicator aligned with magnetic north.
questions.kd: Unit 1.2.9   2.4.2(c) describe the advantages of a directional indicator fitted with a flux valve

questions.question: In which type of lubrication system is an oil scavenge pump essential?
questions.explanation: n a dry sump system, oil is stored in an external tank. The scavenge pump collects oil from the engine sump and returns it to this tank, preventing oil accumulation in the engine.

questions.question: What could be a consequence of a failed scavenge pump in a dry sump system?
options: Oil accumulation in the sump, leading to potential engine damage
questions.explanation: If the scavenge pump fails, oil remains in the engine sump, leading to poor lubrication, increased drag, overheating, and possibly engine failure.

questions.question: Where is the oil cooler bypass valve typically located in the lubrication system?
options: Upstream of the oil pressure pump
Between the oil pressure pump and the oil cooler
questions.explanation: This positioning allows the valve to redirect oil around the cooler when needed, based on temperature conditions.

questions.question: At what condition does the oil cooler bypass valve typically open?
options: When oil temperature is excessively high
When oil viscosity is too low
When oil temperature is below a preset threshold
questions.explanation: The valve is temperature-sensitive, opening when the oil is too cold to prevent overcooling and ensure adequate lubrication.

questions.question: What is a major advantage of electrically driven gyroscopes?
options: They are always lighter than air-driven gyros
They are independent of the aircraft's electrical system
They typically start faster and are less affected by vacuum system limitations
They require a separate hydraulic power source
questions.explanation: Electric gyros generally have quicker spin-up times and avoid reliability issues associated with vacuum or pressure systems.
questions.kd: Unit 1.2.9   2.4.2(a) compare the advantages and disadvantages of air driven and electrically driven gyroscopes

questions.question: The most commonly used extinguishing agent used in a typical aircraft fire extinguishing system is:
questions.explanation: Halon is favored because it is highly effective at interrupting the chemical reaction of a fire, non-conductive, leaves no residue, and works rapidly without damaging sensitive aircraft components.
questions.kd: Unit 1.2.9   2.2.1(g) types of extinguishers and usages

questions.question: Which instrument will overread with a blocked static system whislt on descent?
Which material property in the wire loop helps detect excessive heat?
options: Changes in electrical conductivity or resistance
questions.explanation: The system relies on materials whose electrical properties alter with heat, triggering a fire warning signal.

questions.question: During an ascent you notice that the altimetre is remaining unchanged. The most likely cause is:
options: a small leak in the static line
a large leak in the static line
questions.explanation: The altimeter measures ambient pressure via the static system to determine altitude. If the static system is blocked, then the ambient pressure inside the altimeter remains unchanged, therefore the indicated altitude will also remain unchanged.

questions.question: A main rotor blade out of track will produce:
questions.explanation: A main rotor blade out of track will typically produce a vertical or low-frequency vibration. Rotor blades are said to be “in track” when their tips follow the same path during rotation. If one or more blades are out of track (i.e., one blade flies higher or lower than the others), it creates uneven lift and asymmetry in rotor disc balance.
questions.kd: Unit 1.2.11   2.1.1(b)(i) abnormal vibrations from main and tail toro systems

questions.question: You experience a high frequency vibration during cruise flight, the most likely cause is:
options: tail rotor is out of balance
main rotor is out of balance
questions.explanation: High-frequency vibration (too fast to count or a "buzzing" feel) is usually due to a tail rotor imbalance or tail rotor drive shaft or gearbox issues.
questions.kd: Unit 1.2.11   2.1.1(b)(i) abnormal vibrations from main and tail toro systems

questions.question: In which of the following operational conditions is the formation of carburettor ice most likely
questions.explanation: Carburettor ice is most likely to form under low engine power settings, high humidity, and moderate temperatures (typically between -5°C and +15°C)

questions.question: Which of the following conditions would give the best indication of an abnormally low oil level:
options: Low oil temperature and low oil pressure
High oil temperature and high oil pressure
Low oil temperature and high oil pressure
High oil temperature and low oil pressure
questions.explanation: If the oil level drops too low the oil pump may start to draw air, causing pressure to drop and fluctuate. You might see erratic oil pressure readings, and eventually, low steady pressure. The oil temperature will also rise, but this is usually a secondary indication after pressure drops.

questions.question: A turbocharged engine is started with the waste gate jammed in the closed position. During normal advancement of the throttle after the start, the MP will
options: Increase at a faster than normal rate
Increase at a slower than normal rate
Not increase above the static pressure existing at the time
questions.kd: Unit 1.2.9   2.1.3(c)(iii)(B) waste gate (fixed, manual, and automatic)

questions.question: While cruising at about 5000 ft a helicopter’s pitot tube becomes blocked, but the static vent remains clear. During the descent the Vertical Speed Indicator would show
questions.kd: Unit 1.2.9   2.3.3(b)(i) blockage of the static source

questions.question: A danger of operating an engine at temperatures above the upper limits is that:
options: Some parts may be inadequately lubricated
The oil filter may become blocked

questions.question: During normal advancement of a turbocharged engine, the manifold pressure rises excessively:
options: The waste gate is jammed open
The waste gate is jammed shut
There is too low an oil pressure acting on the piston in the waste gate actuator

questions.question: During descent with the static source blocked:
questions.explanation: The altimeter relies on static pressure to determine altitude. In a descent, outside static pressure increases (because you’re going to lower altitudes). But with a blocked static port, the altimeter is stuck using the last captured pressure — which is lower (from a higher altitude). Therefore, the instrument thinks you're still higher than you are, causing it to overread.

questions.question: In flight you notice a fluctuating CHT gauge:
In level flight with a blocked static vent:
options: The Altimeter will give a correct reading
questions.kd: Unit 1.2.9   2.3.3(b)(i) blockage of the static source

questions.question: In flight you notice a low oil pressure:
options: Oil temperature is too high and parts may become unevenly lubricated
There is a leak in the exhaust manifold
Oil temperature is too low and parts may become unevenly lubricated
The oil cooler bypass is faulty

questions.question: Lead acid type batteries are located in a vented battery box:
options: So that pressure within the battery box can be relieved at high altitude
To ensure the battery is adequately cooled during flight
questions.explanation: Lead-acid batteries are located in vented battery boxes to safely expel hydrogen gas generated during charging and prevent fire or explosion hazards.

questions.question: A correct interpretation of a centre zero ammeter in normal flight is:
options: Negative deflection shows the alternator has failed
Zero indication shows the alternator is changing the battery
Positive deflection shows the battery is providing charge
Zero indication shows the battery is flat
questions.explanation: A center-zero ammeter in an aircraft shows the net current flowing to or from the battery. A negative deflection (below zero) indicates that current is flowing from the battery, which typically means the alternator is not supplying enough power and the battery is discharging.

questions.question: In flight you notice a pronounced 1/REV vibration which is felt as a vertical bounce
options: The main rotor is out of balance
The main rotor is out of track
The main rotor is out of horizontal alignment
questions.explanation: This vibration is typically caused by the main rotor being out of track, which means the rotor blades are not properly aligned with each other.

questions.question: In flight you suspect that carburettor ice is forming. To verify the presence of carburettor ice:
options: Apply full carb heat and note an immediate increase in manifold pressure as the ice melts
Apply partial carb heat and verify presence of ice by noting an immediate decrease in MAP followed by an increase as the ice melts
Apply full carb heat and note a drop in manifold pressure which remains at a lower value
Apply full carb heat and verify presence of ice by noting an immediate decrease in MAP followed by an increase as the ice melts
questions.explanation: To verify the presence of carburettor ice, the pilot can apply full carb heat and observe the manifold pressure (MAP). If carburettor ice is present, applying carb heat will initially cause a decrease in MAP due to the warmer, less dense air entering the engine. As the ice melts, the MAP should then increase back towards normal levels. This sequence of an initial drop followed by a rise in MAP is indicative of carburettor ice melting.
